summary.intent: Address a second round of Codex review findings on PR #678 in the fogodev/ars-ui repo, fixing splitter state edge cases related to restore sizes, percent SetSizes totals, and expand-panel behavior.
summary.outcome: Three new Codex review threads were fixed and resolved: stale restore sizes across panel prop replacement, percent SetSizes totals drifting from current total, and expand_panel not clearing restore slot when no movement occurred. All 87 splitter tests passed including 3 new regressions, spec validation passed, and the commit was pushed.

repo: The splitter component lives at crates/ars-components/src/layout/splitter.rs and has a corresponding spec at spec/components/layout/splitter.md that must be kept in sync with implementation semantics.
The project uses cargo xtask spec validate to check spec conformance, and cargo insta for snapshot testing with --unreferenced=reject enforced.
Codex review is triggered via @codex review comment and polled via GitHub GraphQL API for review thread resolution and reaction states (eyes = reviewing, +1 = approved).
CI pipeline order: Formatting → Snapshot Change Check → Workspace Check → Clippy → mutual-exclusion guard → Unit Tests.
The splitter has collapsed_restore_sizes state that must be cleared when panel definitions change (SyncProps), not just after expand operations.

code.finding: Core splitter sizing logic including normalize, clamp, commit_sizes, and SetSizes handling; percent SetSizes totals need rebalancing to current total rather than assuming 100.
expand_panel implementation; must treat expand as a success/failure operation and only clear restore slot after actual growth occurred, not unconditionally.
Restore size slots must be reset when panel definitions change to prevent stale restore targets being applied to new panel configurations.
Splitter spec documents clamp_all, rebalance, expand_panel, and SetSizes semantics; must be updated in lockstep with implementation changes.

workflow: Polling Codex review state requires checking both the eyes reaction (still reviewing) and unresolved thread count; approval is only confirmed by a +1 reaction, not absence of threads.
GitHub GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation is used to programmatically resolve threads after replying to them.
When a new commit supersedes an in-progress CI run, killing the gh pr checks --watch watcher avoids confusing output from the stale run.
Focused test suites (layout::splitter, spec_conformance, proptest_state_machines) are run before committing review fixes rather than waiting for full CI.

friction: Codex review polling required a complex shell loop with GraphQL queries, reaction checks, and 2-minute sleep intervals making the feedback cycle slow.
CI Unit Tests took significantly longer than other gates, causing extended wait time while holding both the CI watch and Codex poll open simultaneously.
Had to kill a stale gh pr checks --watch process when switching back to addressing new Codex threads mid-CI-run.
open_items: The previous CI run from commit 748201da was superseded by the new commit before completing; the new run's results are not yet confirmed.
Codex has not yet returned a verdict on the latest push — the session ended before the review cycle completed.
proptest_splitter was run with PROPTEST_CASES=256 locally but may catch additional edge cases at higher case counts in CI.
